planting.note: Direct sow shallowly in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun in spring after last frost; tansy spreads aggressively by rhizomes and self-seeding and is best confined by root barriers in garden borders or planted in naturalistic areas where spreading is acceptable.

directions: Tanacetum vulgare is a vigorous, aromatic perennial producing deeply divided, fern-like, dark-green leaves with an intensely camphor-like scent and flat-topped clusters of bright, button-yellow flower heads in late summer that were historically planted near doorways to repel flies, ants, and fleas; the dried flowers hold their color well and are valuable for dried arrangements. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested in significant quantities; it contains thujone, a neurotoxic compound, and should not be consumed internally. Do not grow near vegetables or edible crops as the allelopathic compounds can inhibit germination.

planting.note: Direct sow on the surface (seeds need light) in dry, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, poor to average soil in full sun; wormwood thrives in lean conditions and will become rank and floppy in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Artemisia absinthium is a silver-white, intensely aromatic shrubby perennial with silky, deeply dissected, ghostly pale foliage that makes it one of the most striking foliage plants in the dry garden; the bitter compounds (including thujone) were historically used medicinally as an anthelmintic (worm expeller), digestive bitter, and febrifuge, and are the source of the bitterness in vermouth and historically in absinthe. The foliage is strongly allelopathic — plant away from edible crops. The silver-gray foliage is an outstanding foil for purple-flowered companions like salvias and catmints.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface (seeds require light to germinate) after last frost in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sweet wormwood grows extremely fast and will reach 6–8 feet in a single season — allow adequate space.

directions: Artemisia annua is a strongly camphor-scented annual from China that has become globally important as the source of artemisinin, the active compound in the most effective modern antimalarial drugs; the plant is now grown at large scale throughout Africa and Asia for pharmaceutical production. In gardens it is grown as an intensely fragrant ornamental annual with feathery, bright-green foliage and masses of tiny yellow flower heads in late summer; the dried branches retain their sweet, camphorous scent for months and are used in dried arrangements, herbal sachets, and potpourri. It self-seeds prolifically in mild climates.
sources: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; unlike most artemisias, white mugwort requires consistent moisture and shade tolerance makes it the best Artemisia for partially shaded positions.

directions: Artemisia lactiflora is exceptional among artemisias for its large, deeply divided, rich-green (not silver) foliage and for its tall panicles of creamy-white, softly fragrant flower plumes in late summer and fall that closely resemble astilbe or meadow rue; the flowers are excellent for cutting. It is the go-to artemisia for partially shaded mixed borders and is not invasive like many other species; it requires staking in windy positions and produces its best display in reliably moist, fertile soil enriched with compost. The cultivar 'Guizhou' has dark-purple-flushed foliage that is especially ornamental.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in average, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mugwort spreads aggressively by rhizomes and self-sowing and can become invasive — it is listed as a noxious weed in some states, so check local regulations before planting and use root barriers or plant in wild areas away from manicured gardens.

directions: Artemisia vulgaris is one of the most widespread and ancient medicinal herbs in the world, used across Europe, Asia, and the Americas for digestive complaints, menstrual regulation, and as a flavoring in traditional European and Korean cuisine; the leaves have a distinctive aromatic, slightly bitter flavor and are traditionally used to stuff roast goose or used in moxibustion in traditional Chinese medicine. The silver-backed, deeply dissected foliage is attractive, and the plant is highly drought-tolerant once established; however, it is potentially invasive and should be managed carefully.
